answer:
You know, honestly, I think people are so afraid of making mistakes because there's this constant pressure to seem competent, especially when you're trying to achieve something important. For me, as someone who's applying to universities in the U.S., I always feel like every little error could, I don't know, impact how others view my abilities. A lot of us have grown up in environments where mistakes are kinda treated as failures, rather than opportunities to grow or learn, which, in my opinion, makes us way more cautious than we should be.

Also, I think social media plays a role in this. You see people posting only their successes or their perfect moments online, and it's easy to imagine that everyone else is just getting things right all the time. So when you mess up, it feels really isolating, like you’re the only one not living up to expectations. I mean, I’ve had times when I hesitated to speak up in class or join activities because I was worried about doing something wrong and being judged. It seriously creates a ton of stress and makes you second-guess yourself.

But honestly, I’ve realized that if you never make mistakes, you probably aren’t really challenging yourself or stepping out of your comfort zone. Mistakes are inevitable, and they’re kind of essential if you want to improve or discover what you’re genuinely good at. So, even though the fear makes sense, I think it’s important to reframe how we view mistakes—like, they shouldn’t define us, but instead just help us learn and become more resilient.
title: Everyone makes mistakes, but why do you think that many people are so afraid of making them? Explain your reasoning.
